bookCover.coverDescription:
At Launch, we blend executive function coaching with academic support to help students build the skills they need to thrive—both in school and in life.

This training will guide you through:

  • Our mission and approach
  • Information on EF and ADHD
  • The structure of a Launch session
  • Best practices for supporting students

By the end, you’ll be ready to help students develop EF skills, improve academic performance, and grow into independent learners. 

Let’s get started.

params.alt: StudyHouse: Academic Tutoring & Executive Skills Coaching
params.title: StudyHouse Education

params.text: Launch is an educational service that blends executive function coaching with academic content and study skills support. The goal of this service is to help students become better at managing their workloads and learn to effectively execute a homework session, while also helping them improve academic performance.

We want to lead students down a path of healthy homework habits and teach them that when they are deliberate about planning, prioritizing, and executing their work, several rewards come with it: 

  • improved grades
  • less fighting with parents
  • free time spent without homework hanging over their heads. 

While helping students foster growth in executive and academic domains is the primary goal, we also need to consider ourselves as the backstops for these students.

Many of them do not have the skills yet to effectively manage their work without support, so we need to be there to:

  • catch late or missing work, and make a plan to complete it
  • understand test performances and look for makeup opportunities
  • make sure all assignments and grades are accounted for

choices.question: What skill(s) do we want to emphasize during our Launch sessions?
answers: Create and execute a plan to complete assignments

params.text:
What is ADHD?

ADHD is a neurodevelopmental condition that affects attention, impulse control, and self-regulation. Some core traits include:

Inattention – forgetfulness, distractibility, losing track of tasks  

Hyperactivity – restlessness, difficulty staying seated 

Impulsivity – interrupting, rushing through work without checking

ADHD & Executive Function (EF):

ADHD is closely tied to EF — the brain’s “management system” for planning, prioritizing, and completing tasks.

  • Students with ADHD often struggle with:
    • Starting tasks
    • Organizing materials/work
    • Remembering steps or instructions
    • Managing time
    • Following through to completion

Why This Matters for Launch: 

Many of our students can succeed academically but need scaffolding around EF.

 

Our role: support the EF scaffolding until students can internalize these skills

metadata.title: What is ADHD and how does it relate to EF?

params.text: What are some EF skills we focus on?
The ability to feel, identify, and regulate emotions, and well as adapt to their social environment.
The ability to sustain or shift attention in response to different demands and rules in various settings.
The ability to evaluate our own experience, feelings, and behavior in the moment.
The ability to look forward and make decisions, consider the consequences of actions, and juggle multiple tasks.
The ability to get started and take steps toward reaching a goal.
The ability to maintain information and items in a way that makes reaching a goal easier.
The ability to keep track of how much time has passed and estimate how long tasks will take.

In this way, we serve as the executive part of their brains while helping them develop their own skills through coaching, building rapport and student connection, modeling, direct instruction, and encouraging students to buy into the process.

Launch Session: 

What does it look like?

We can break down a Launch session into four components:

 

  1.  Development of ‘Workload Awareness’
  2.  Prioritization of work and forming a plan of attack
  3.  Execution of work/content support
  4.  ‘Summary and Work Completion’ Plan Email

 

TRUST BUT VERIFY through all components of the service. This means if a student tells you they completed and submitted an assignment, we need to verify it.

Step 1: Develop workload awareness

When beginning the process of developing workload awareness, you must sign into the student’s:

  1. Learning Management System (LMS) such as Schoology, Canvas, Google Classroom
  2. Grades (SIS)
  3. Google Drive and Gmail ‘username@fcpsschools.net’

 

 How aware is the student of their workload? 
 

The Planning Sheet: Make the workload visual (externalize) 
 

Teachers post assignments in all sorts of places, so getting them in one place to visualize is important. We use our custom planning sheet to achieve this.

Sources for building workload awareness:

  • Login to whichever LMS they use (Schoology, Canvas, etc.) and look for:
    • Folders (typically labeled by Unit, then by week)
    • PowerPoints (HW is often posted on the last slide of PowerPoint!)
    • Calendars or schedules
    • Don't just look at the sidebar To-Do list!
    • Dig dig dig for assignments and what is going on in class
  • Third-party websites (DeltaMath, Mathspace, Spanish websites “This Is School”)
    • You can access these sites using the internal links in their Schoology folders, and log in with the student emails

• Checking Grades (SIS)
    • Check grades at the start of every session
    • Look for missing work, poor test scores, anything that needs to be addressed.
    • Look for the good stuff and acknowledge it!
    • Report to Colton any students who have a D or F
    • Can also be a source for upcoming assignments
    • Grades can be accessed with the same login credentials as Schoology

Not all teachers are organized or well-versed in the technology within the LMS, so it is important to check any handouts given in class (i.e., ask to see the student’s binder, folder, notes packets, backpack, etc.)

Ask the students! What else do you know you have that is due this week?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This helps both you and the student understand where they are in each course so they can allocate their time and effort accordingly. It is also a smart way to check for missing assignments and ways to bring up their grade using upcoming ones.

answers.text: Check through their SIS grades together at the beginning of each session

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Having it visually laid out is extremely helpful for teaching students how to organize their workload. It helps them keep track and also gives them accountability for their work.

answers.text: Find their assignments in Schoology and show it to them through the planning sheet

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Important! Not everything is on their LMS - some classworks, homeworks, and projects are on paper, so it is a *must* to ask about this each time!

answers.text: Check their binders and ask if they got any handouts in class

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. This is not productive and doesn't help strengthen their EF skillset.

params.question: How can we help students be aware of their workload?

params.text:
Step 2: Prioritization and Developing a Plan of Attack

Involve the students and discuss how to prioritize the workload. You want to help them learn how to think about prioritization, a skill they will eventually develop as their own.

How do we plan and prioritize work by assignment type and with their strengths?

Things to consider:

  • Weight of the assignment (formative, summative; how many points is it worth?)
  • Understand the student’s strengths and weaknesses:
    • Use your judgment to determine what to complete here, then prepare them to tackle the rest at home

  • Timeliness and difficulty (when is it due? how long does it take to finish?)

    • Mark the due dates and prioritize the work accordingly

Then, discuss the “ plan of attack ” with the student. In reality, the details of this discussion will vary from student to student. It may range from laying out a plan for each day of the week for the student to just an informal discussion. 
 

Always explain how to study for tests - never just say “ study for ______.”

  • Consider resources available for studying for tests and how they will study.

Once the workload is made clear and the prioritization is discussed, write down the tasks the student wishes to complete during the session and for the rest of the week on the physical to-do list. They take this list home.

  • We revisit this step at the end of the session (Step 4)

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This will visually aid the student in understanding their workload and help them organize it into smaller, digestible parts

answers.text: Mark the due dates for upcoming assignments in the planning sheet

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Make use of their time at StudyHouse and be a good resource for them, which includes preparing them with enough information to finish any incomplete work at home

answers.text: Spend more time together on assignments that you think they will struggle with doing alone at home

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: For a student who has several missing assignments, it would be smarter to score higher on the next summative (which weighs more) than attempt to complete all the missing work in a tight deadline

answers.text: If they're performing poorly in a course, focus on an upcoming summative versus a homework assignment

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect - this is the easy way out and doesn't accurately consider the student's strengths/weaknesses, their grade in the course, or their ability to complete the work at home
tipsAndFeedback.tip: Would we really make it that easy?

answers.text: Just follow the order in the to-do list on Schoology

params.question: What are some ways we help prioritize work with our student?

params.text:
Step 3: Execute the plan of attack

Teach toward independence—don’t get sucked into one student, and look for opportunities for students to work on their own.

How can I scaffold task initiation to build students’ capacity for independent work? 

For example, if writing a paper, work together to develop the thesis, then guide them through one body paragraph. After that, let them try the next one on their own, and then provide them with immediate feedback. 

In math or science, work example problems together until they gain an understanding, then let them start the problem set on their own, and you can be there to answer questions along the way.

Quality control: It is important to check for quality before students submit assignments. Math/science work completed at StudyHouse should always be checked for accuracy and understanding to the best of our ability. For humanities, check for:

  • thoroughness
  • accuracy
  • capitalization and general proofreading
     

Follow the rubric/assignment directions: Make it a point to show the student that you are checking every aspect of the directions, so that they also become aware of the expectations.

Paper Writing: do not overstep! It is more important to help the student revise the flow of ideas and make the paper more organized/adhere to the rubric, rather than make the writing sound more sophisticated.

Avoid baby birding - refrain from giving small pieces of help over time that lead students to answer without generating their own ideas. A goal of ours is to foster independent thought and problem-solving skills rather than create a dependency on the tutor. 

  • Ask guiding questions: What do you think the next step is?
  • Encourage trial and error: The process of failing and learning from mistakes typically sticks with them

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: For STEM, having the first problem worked out with them will help them remember the steps once they start one on their own.

answers.text: Start by leading example problems then let them practice on their own

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is the best way for students to figure out how to write independently. They will synthesize their own writing using your example along the way.

answers.text: Write the thesis together, show them how to organize one body paragraph, then let them use that as a guideline for the rest

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect: Let students brainstorm their own ideas and try to see their perspective. Work with what they have and map it out with them. Then, narrow it down together and offer advice.

answers.text: Correct them in every step of their writing process and give them your own ideas

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No - you can't expect the student to somehow start a problem and know how to solve it if they haven't had the chance to ever tackle it alone. It is important to let them "fail" here so you can gently correct and adjust. This way, they will also learn from their mistakes and build the independence.

answers.text: Do all problems together here and expect them to figure out the rest on their own at home

params.question: What can we do to help students work independently and accurately?

params.text:
After the Launch Session:

What do I have to do after my student leaves?

Step 4: Summary and work completion email

The purpose of this email is to briefly recap the work done during tutoring and outline the plan of action developed in Step 2 of the service. By utilizing the “Mail Send” feature on the planning sheet, emails will automatically be sent to the summary email (summaries@cmteducation.com) and the student’s parents (must be filled out on the planning sheet).

  • Ideally, the student and tutor write this email together
  • The email states what was completed during the Launch session
  • The email breaks down the remaining work and plan of attack that was discussed with the student (see example below)
    • For example, not all students will require a day-to-day plan, or some may have less work than others. Include any reminders or information you think they will need: any resources to help them complete the remaining work on their own, any upcoming exams/relevant deadlines, or any goals before the next session.

To send the summary email, use our template and modify as needed. To generate the draft email, navigate to the “Mail Send” feature using these directions:


 

 

 

 

 

 


 

For Launch, type in “1” in the pop-up box. For a private, type in “2”. Then, press “OK”. You should see the summary draft generated in your “@cmteducation.com” email account.

params.solutionText:
Acknowledge the student’s emotions first and reduce demands before academics. I would say something like, “It sounds like today has been really hard. We don’t have to jump into work right away. I'm going to give you a few minutes, okay?” I might offer a brief grounding option (breathing, snack, water) and wait until the student is calmer before introducing the task.

What this shows:
Emotional regulation comes before productivity; avoids power struggles.
params.taskDescription:
Dysregulation / Emotional Overload

A student arrives to the session visibly upset. They put their head down, say “I hate this class,” and refuse to open their laptop.

What is your first response in this situation, and why?

metadata.title: Respond to the following scenario to the best of your ability.

params.solutionText:
Validate the feeling without agreeing to task avoidance, then reframe. For example: “Yeah, this does look boring. Let’s figure out the fastest way to get through it together.” I might introduce a time limit or mini-goal to create momentum.

What this shows:
Validation + structure, not dismissal or lecturing.
params.taskDescription:
Low Motivation / “This Is Boring”

A student says, “This is pointless and boring,” and starts scrolling on their phone instead of working.

How do you redirect the student while validating their feelings and maintaining session goals?

metadata.title: Respond to the following scenario to the best of your ability.

params.solutionText:
Set clear boundaries while staying calm and supportive. I might say, “AI can help with ideas, but it can’t do the thinking for us. We can use it to brainstorm, and then you put it in your own words.” I would work with the student to create an outline, then monitor them as they write their own response independently.

What this shows:
Academic integrity + realistic expectations on AI usage.
params.taskDescription:
Use of AI During the Session

A student pastes an AI-generated response into their assignment and says, “It’s fine, my teacher doesn’t care.”

How do you respond while maintaining academic integrity and supporting skill development?

metadata.title: Respond to the following scenario to the best of your ability.

params.solutionText:
Focus on effort, regulation, or strategy rather than output. For example: “Even though we didn’t finish much, you stayed with it and asked for help. We got this next time!” I’d briefly preview the next step so the student leaves feeling oriented, not behind.

What this shows:
Growth mindset and executive functioning reinforcement.
params.taskDescription:
End-of-Session Reflection

The session is almost over, and the student completed very little work but stayed regulated.

How do you close the session in a way that reinforces progress and supports future success?

metadata.title: Respond to the following scenario to the best of your ability.

noChapterInteractionBoldText: You have not interacted with any pages.
noChapterInteractionText: You have to interact with at least one page before you can see the summary.
